structured_choices.data_source: Event-level clickstream logs from production e-commerce platform spanning 6 months (e.g., Jan–Jun). Raw data includes page views, searches, add-to-cart events, dwell time, device type, referral source, timestamps, session IDs, user IDs, and binary purchase indicator within 7 days post-session. Minimum session length: 2 events. Data quality: flag and exclude sessions with incomplete timestamps, null user IDs, or malformed event sequences; document exclusion rates.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label per session: 1 if user completed ≥1 purchase within 7 calendar days after session end timestamp; 0 otherwise. Label is derived from transaction records joined to clickstream on user ID and purchase date. A session is the unit of analysis; multiple sessions per user are possible and handled via stratification (see split_method).
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with no user-level leakage. Training set: Jan–Mar (12 weeks). Validation set: Apr (4 weeks, non-overlapping users if possible; if user overlap unavoidable, stratify validation by user to detect leakage). Test set: May–Jun (8 weeks, held-out until final evaluation). Justification: Temporal ordering prevents the model from learning future information, respects concept drift from seasonal/promotional effects, and aligns with realistic deployment scenario where model is trained on historical data and evaluated on future holdout. Non-overlapping users between train and val/test is preferred but documented if infeasible; in that case, stratified sampling by user ID ensures each user's sessions are concentrated in one split to minimize data leakage. Time-based split is essential for time-series data and conversion prediction where recency and session order matter.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (feature scalers, categorical encoders, sequence padding/truncation lengths, vocabulary for embedding lookups) are fit exclusively on the training set (Jan–Mar). Fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents label leakage and ensures the model does not have access to statistics from future data. Specifically: (1) Sequence length distribution is computed on train data to set padding/truncation parameters; (2) Continuous features (dwell time, recency) are scaled (e.g., StandardScaler or robust scaler) fit on train; (3) Categorical features (device type, referral source) are encoded (one-hot or ordinal) fit on train vocabulary; (4) Any imputation of missing event sequences is done using train statistics.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed to enable fair comparison. Baseline features (aggregated, non-sequential): (a) Session event counts (total events, page views, searches, add-to-cart), (b) Recency (days since last user session before current session), (c) Device type and referral source (categorical, one-hot encoded), (d) Session duration (minutes from first to last event). Treatment features (sequential embeddings): (a) All baseline features, plus (b) Temporally windowed session embedding derived from a sequence encoder (e.g., bidirectional LSTM or Transformer) applied to the clickstream event sequence within the session, with events ordered chronologically and encoded as (event_type, dwell_time, relative_timestamp). The embedding is the final hidden state or mean pooling over event encodings. Window size is fixed (e.g., last 50 events or all events in session, whichever is shorter). No leakage checks: event types and dwell times are observed during session, not future purchase information; embedding is computed from session content alone.
structured_choices.model: Treatment model: Gradient boosting classifier (e.g., XGBoost, LightGBM) or neural network (feed-forward MLP or shallow CNN on flattened embeddings + aggregated features). The model takes baseline features + session embedding as input and outputs a purchase probability. Embedding is generated by a learned or fixed sequence encoder (e.g., LSTM trained end-to-end or pre-trained embedding model, depending on compute constraints). Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (see validation_approach).
structured_choices.baseline: Gradient boosting classifier (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on baseline features only (session counts, recency, device, referral source). The baseline is given equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort as the treatment model (grid or random search on same validation data, same metric). Both models use the same feature preprocessing pipeline and evaluation protocol. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of the sequence embedding, not differences in model tuning or training regime.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Justification: Conversion is typically imbalanced (e.g., 5–15% positive rate in e-commerce), so AUROC can be misleading. AUPRC directly reflects the business goal of identifying high-intent sessions: it measures precision (reduction in wasted outreach) and recall (capture rate of eventual purchasers) across decision thresholds. For retargeting and intervention campaigns, practitioners care about the trade-off between targeting efficiency and reach, which AUPRC captures. Additionally, AUPRC is threshold-agnostic and suitable for ranking/scoring downstream systems.
secondary_metrics: AUROC (to detect if relative ranking of all sessions improves)
Lift at top-k (e.g., precision@10% of sessions, recall@10% of sessions) — operationally relevant for retargeting budget constraints
Calibration (Brier score, expected calibration error) — ensures the model's predicted probabilities match empirical conversion rates, important for decision-making
Per-user metrics (e.g., mean reciprocal rank of first positive session per user) — to check if the model improves ranking within individual user journeys
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (Apr) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Both baseline and treatment models are trained on the training set (Jan–Mar) multiple times with different hyperparameter configurations. For each configuration, validation AUPRC is computed on the validation set. The configuration that maximizes validation AUPRC is selected. The test set (May–Jun) is held-out and used only once, at the end, to report final metrics (AUPRC, AUROC, lift, calibration). No tuning or model selection occurs on the test set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and used only for final evaluation after all model selection is complete. It is evaluated once. Results reported are point estimates and confidence intervals (bootstrap 95% CI) computed on the test set, stratified by time period (May vs Jun) to check for temporal stability. The test set is never used for hyperparameter search, cross-validation, or threshold selection.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Multiple confounds are addressed: (1) User-level leakage: Train/val/test are split temporally and users are stratified such that each user's sessions appear in only one split where possible; if overlap is unavoidable, this is flagged and documented. (2) Campaign and temporal effects: A separate sensitivity analysis evaluates model performance stratified by promotion periods (flagged in metadata) and non-promotion periods to ensure gains are not driven by campaign-specific signals. (3) Near-duplicate sessions: Sessions are deduplicated on (user_id, session_start_time) to remove accidental repeats; if a user has multiple sessions within 7 days, each is retained as independent (they are not leakage, they are distinct behavior). (4) Concept drift: Performance is reported separately for early test (May) and late test (Jun) to detect degradation over time; if drift is large, a retraining schedule is recommended. (5) Holdout population check: A small stratified holdout of users is set aside before any modeling (not used for train, val, or test); this population is scored by both models post-hoc to verify generalization claims are not an artifact of the train/val/test populations.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Temporally Windowed Session Embeddings for Conversion Prediction

### Objective
To test whether a model incorporating learned embeddings from clickstream event sequences outperforms a baseline model built from session-level aggregates in predicting 7-day purchase conversion, while controlling for temporal confounds and data leakage.

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We will extract 6 months of event-level clickstream logs (January through June) from the production platform. Each row represents a single event (page view, search, add-to-cart, etc.) with timestamp, event type, session ID, user ID, device type, referral source, and dwell time. Sessions are the unit of analysis; a session is a sequence of events from one user within a contiguous time window (e.g., 30 minutes of inactivity ends a session).

Labels are derived by joining sessions to transaction records: a session receives label 1 if the user completed at least one purchase within 7 calendar days after the session ended; otherwise, label 0. Exclude sessions with incomplete timestamps, null IDs, or malformed sequences. Document the exclusion rate and the overall positive rate.

### 2. Train/Validation/Test Split

We employ a temporal split with user-level stratification to prevent leakage:
- **Training set:** January–March (12 weeks)
- **Validation set:** April (4 weeks)
- **Test set:** May–June (8 weeks)

Justification: Temporal ordering prevents the model from using future information, respects concept drift from seasonality and promotions, and reflects real deployment where the model is trained on historical data and evaluated on unseen future periods. 

User-level stratification is applied to minimize cross-contamination: when feasible, each user's sessions appear in only one split. If user overlap is unavoidable (common in long-running platforms), we stratify the validation and test sets by user ID and document the overlap rate. This reduces (though does not eliminate) the risk of correlated sessions leaking signal across splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature sets are constructed:

**Baseline features (non-sequential):**
- Session event counts: total events, page views, searches, add-to-cart actions
- Recency: number of days since the user's prior session
- Device type and referral source (one-hot encoded)
- Session duration (minutes)

**Treatment features (sequential embeddings):**
- All baseline features, *plus*
- A learned embedding vector derived from the session's clickstream sequence. Events are ordered chronologically, encoded as (event_type, dwell_time, relative_timestamp), and passed through a sequence encoder (e.g., bidirectional LSTM or Transformer). The output embedding is the final hidden state or mean pooling over event representations. Sequence length is fixed (e.g., padded/truncated to 50 events).

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, sequence length parameters) are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents label leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Treatment model:** A gradient boosting classifier (XGBoost or LightGBM) or neural network (MLP or CNN) trained on baseline features + session embedding, end-to-end with the sequence encoder.

**Baseline model:** The same classifier (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on baseline features only.

Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: grid or random search over learning rate, regularization, tree depth, etc., evaluated on the validation set. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of the sequence embedding, not differential tuning.

### 5. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are selected to maximize AUPRC on the validation set (April). The test set (May–June) is held-out and used once, at the end, for final reporting only.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Conversion is typically imbalanced (5–15% positive rate), so AUPRC is more informative than AUROC. It directly reflects the business objective: precision quantifies wasted outreach reduction, recall quantifies purchaser capture, and their trade-off drives retargeting and intervention ROI.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUROC (overall ranking quality)
- Lift at top-k (e.g., precision and recall when targeting the top 10% of sessions by score)
- Calibration (Brier score, expected calibration error)
- Per-user metrics (mean reciprocal rank of first positive session per user)

### 7. Confound Controls

**User-level leakage:** Addressed via temporal and stratified splits; overlap is flagged and documented.

**Campaign effects:** Perform sensitivity analysis stratifying by promotion vs. non-promotion periods (metadata flagged in raw data). If gains are concentrated in promotion periods, the model may be exploiting campaign signals rather than learning sequence behavior.

**Concept drift:** Report performance separately for early test (May) and late test (June). Large drops indicate drift; recommend retraining schedules if observed.

**Holdout population:** Set aside a small stratified holdout of users before modeling. Score both models on this population post-hoc to verify generalization.

### 8. Interpretation and Claims

The experiment will establish whether the treatment model achieves higher AUPRC on the held-out test set. It will *not* establish causality or interpretability of the learned embeddings. Any gain could come from increased model capacity; ablations (e.g., training a baseline neural network on the same architecture) are recommended to isolate the value of sequences.
